_doc: Real 3D-printing businesses surfaced as inspiration during validation. Matched to user's category by `categories` overlap, with `theme` fallbacks for cross-category lessons. Curated, not exhaustive.

businesses.name: Wooj
businesses.summary: Brooklyn print farm that scaled from the viral pandemic-era Wavy Lamp into a human-centric design brand.
businesses.differentiator: Brand storytelling + restraint — reuses premade lighting hardware across SKUs instead of redesigning every part from scratch. Founder builds in public via YouTube Shorts.

businesses.name: Crème Atelier
businesses.summary: Made-to-order Soft Serve Lamp in recycled rPLA from Dutch food packaging, ~6h print time per unit.
businesses.differentiator: Dezeen-shortlisted design language is the moat, not the printer. Model: design IP > production IP.

businesses.name: Gantri
businesses.summary: Designer-led lighting platform using proprietary plant-based polymers. Brings in independent designers (Joey Zeledon, BEBOP) and handles all production.
businesses.differentiator: Marketplace model — they're both the print farm AND the curator, so each new designer compounds catalogue depth.

businesses.name: Nervous System
businesses.summary: Generative-algorithmic jewelry, lamps and puzzles. Hyphae Lamp $330, Convolution Bangle $400.
businesses.differentiator: Customers buy the algorithm, not just the object. The codebase IS the moat.

businesses.name: Sheyn
businesses.summary: Vint//16 Blend Vase and similar pieces in dual-tone PLA gradient — every piece visually unique.
businesses.differentiator: Uses the printing process itself as marketing — the gradient is the photograph, not an afterthought.

businesses.name: FRANCA Studio
businesses.summary: Made-to-order pendant lights launched in 2023.
businesses.differentiator: Positions PLA finishes using fashion vocabulary — 'organza, linen, satin' — borrowing premium textile language for plastic.

businesses.name: JK3D (Julia Koerner)
businesses.summary: Flora Decanter (sand-printed base + hand-blown glass) and other limited pieces from a solar-powered lab.
businesses.differentiator: Hollywood credentials + sustainable production = clear premium positioning at gallery price points.

businesses.name: Lucas Zito
businesses.summary: Modular bioplastic lamps sold through Rossana Orlandi gallery in Milan.
businesses.differentiator: Collectible-design tier — sells through galleries, not a webshop. The distribution channel signals the price tier.

businesses.name: UAU Project
businesses.summary: Lamps and vases in colourful FDM PLA.
businesses.differentiator: Leans INTO visible FDM layer lines as a tactile design feature instead of trying to hide them.

businesses.name: Mediterranea Design
businesses.scale: Product arm of digital-fab lab Medaarch
businesses.summary: Furniture printed in PLA with a 'Made in Italy' provenance story.
businesses.differentiator: Mediterranean naming and provenance storytelling justify European-craft pricing on PLA furniture.

businesses.name: Model No.
businesses.summary: Custom outdoor and indoor furniture from plant-based bio-resins paired with FSC hardwood.
businesses.differentiator: Online configurator — the customer co-designs the piece before it's printed, so every order is bespoke and committed.

businesses.name: Aectual
businesses.scale: Clients: Lululemon, Tiffany & Co, Schiphol, Nike Town London
businesses.summary: Spun out of DUS Architects. Planters and dividers from recycled beverage cartons (PolyAl).
businesses.differentiator: B2B / architectural channel + a Circular Take Back program turn corporate sustainability mandates into orders.

businesses.name: The New Raw
businesses.summary: X-Bench (~55kg of recycled plastic per unit) and a Zero Waste Lab in Thessaloniki where citizens bring plastic to be turned into local street furniture.
businesses.differentiator: Turns the recycling story into a participatory civic project — the lab itself is the marketing.

businesses.name: Reform Design Lab
businesses.summary: Lounge Chair printed sideways in DuraSense wood-fibre biocomposite, giving a 'ribbon' surface texture.
businesses.differentiator: Signature material + visible print direction = instantly recognisable from a single photograph.

businesses.name: Nagami
businesses.scale: Pieces in Centre Pompidou & Vitra Design Museum
businesses.summary: Tumbonas chairs and limited pieces. Collaborators include Zaha Hadid Architects and Ross Lovegrove.
businesses.differentiator: Museum-grade collectible-art tier — competing on architectural pedigree, not on price or print speed.

businesses.name: Goodsmith
businesses.summary: 3D-printed glue-on horseshoes in TPU, modular system for horses with hoof or leg issues.
businesses.differentiator: Niche dominance — solving an expensive recurring problem in a market still using 2,000-year-old iron tech.

businesses.name: Hoet
businesses.summary: Couture titanium frames laser-printed (DMLS) in 0.03mm layers.
businesses.differentiator: Heritage brand + tech moat — lattice geometries impossible without metal printing become the visible signature.

businesses.name: Zellerfeld
businesses.summary: Custom-fit shoes via a phone foot-scan, single-material TPU print, no glue or stitching, fully recyclable.
businesses.differentiator: Shoes-as-a-platform — designers manufacture through Zellerfeld's stack instead of building their own factory.

businesses.name: LACE by Jenny Wu
businesses.summary: DMLS rings from silver to 18K gold.
businesses.differentiator: 'Wearable architecture' positioning attracts design-minded buyers who'd never browse a traditional jewellery counter.

businesses.name: OLA Jewelry
businesses.summary: Belgian minimalist jewellery in nylon up to gold-plated steel.
businesses.differentiator: Accessible entry-price tier in a market dominated by premium pricing — captures buyers locked out of higher-tier brands.

businesses.name: CruiseCup
businesses.scale: ~$300K revenue in a single month (Business Insider)
businesses.summary: 3D-printed beverage and can holders, sold direct via Shopify.
businesses.differentiator: Viral Instagram/TikTok content as the engine — close to 1M views per launch — funnelling straight to checkout.

businesses.name: Wigglitz (ZB Designs)
businesses.scale: $18M revenue 2025, 1.5M units in December alone, ~2,700+ Bambu Lab X1C printers
businesses.summary: Collectible articulated figurines now in Target via a Moose Toys partnership. Claims the largest US print farm.
businesses.differentiator: Collector-loop product (200+ figurines) + 'Made in USA' premium against $0.60 Chinese alternatives.

businesses.name: Hero Forge / Sky Castle Studios
businesses.summary: In-browser character creator that prints D&D miniatures from polymer up to bronze keepsakes.
businesses.differentiator: The software is the product — printing and fulfilment are outsourced to Shapeways. Pure design-tool moat.

businesses.name: Solefully
businesses.summary: 3D-printed Croc charms, the trend-rider success that funded CruiseCup.
businesses.differentiator: Classic trend-rider playbook — pick a hot host product (Crocs) and make accessories for it. Now winding down.

businesses.name: Slant 3D
businesses.scale: 1,000+ printer farm. Customers reportedly include Amazon and Nickelodeon.
businesses.summary: Print-farm-as-a-service plus SlantPod and Teleport (a Shopify→print-farm integration so brands skip building their own farm).
businesses.differentiator: Vertical integration — the production layer underneath everyone else's brand. Their YouTube channel is also their lead-gen.

businesses.name: i-SOLIDS
businesses.scale: ~140 printers (64 Flashforge Creator Pros running 6+ years), expanding to 400 in 20,000 sqft
businesses.summary: Service bureau with extreme machine utilisation.
businesses.differentiator: Squeezes the maximum lifetime out of older proven hardware running near-100% utilisation for years — an ROI lesson, not a hardware-flex story.

businesses.name: Forust (by Desktop Metal)
businesses.summary: 3D prints with sawdust as feedstock — replicates oak, teak and walnut grain. Used for planters, light fixtures and wall blocks.
businesses.differentiator: Novel material story (waste from the furniture industry → new furniture) is itself the marketing.

businesses.name: Manu Matters
businesses.summary: Turns lemon peels, PET bottles and cornstarch into homeware (~$250 lamps and vases).
businesses.differentiator: 'Adopt this object' emotional framing — every product is a sustainability story the buyer can repeat to friends.
